L1.area: Command execution approval and process invocation infrastructure

L2.description: A race condition vulnerability exists in the command execution approval flow for node-hosted processes. Between the time a command execution request is approved and when it is actually executed, an attacker can rebind a mutable parent symlink in the working directory path, causing the approved command to execute from a different filesystem location than what was validated at approval time. The approval forwarding logic also trusts caller-supplied parameter values instead of using immutable approval-time canonical values, allowing the execution context to drift from what was originally approved.

L3.area: Node-hosted command execution approval hardening and approval-time working directory validation
L3.description: A race condition vulnerability exists in the node-hosted command execution approval flow. After a command's working directory is validated and the execution request is approved, an attacker can rebind a mutable parent symlink component in the cwd path before execution occurs, redirecting the approved command to a different filesystem location while the visible cwd string remains unchanged. The approval forwarding and sanitization logic trusts caller-supplied parameter values for argv, cwd, agentId, and sessionKey rather than enforcing immutable approval-time canonical plan values, allowing the execution context to be altered after approval. This enables a TOCTOU attack where the filesystem state inspected at approval time diverges from the state at execution time.

ground_truth.reason: The command execution approval flow does not reject mutable parent-symlink path components in the working directory and trusts caller-supplied parameters during approval forwarding instead of enforcing immutable approval-time canonical plan values, allowing an attacker to rebind a parent symlink between approval and execution to redirect command execution to an arbitrary filesystem location.
